description: Rules for generating executable macOS apps (SwiftUI / AppKit) via Swift Playground. Focused on making the generated binary actually appear as a visible, interactive window — not a headless background process.

guidelines.rule: Every SwiftUI @main macOS app MUST call NSApplication.shared.setActivationPolicy(.regular) AND NSApplication.shared.activate(ignoringOtherApps: true) in its init(). Import AppKit at the top of the file.
guidelines.why: When the binary is launched directly (e.g. `swift run` or `.build/.../MyApp`) instead of from a .app bundle, macOS treats it as a command-line tool. The default activation policy gives no Dock tile, no menu bar presence, and windows that stay hidden behind every other app with no way to bring them forward. Users repeatedly report 'the app ran but nothing showed up' because the window exists but is invisible. This is the SINGLE most common failure of generated macOS apps — and it wastes the whole point of generating them.
examples: REQUIRED pattern: `@main struct MyApp: App { init() { NSApplication.shared.setActivationPolicy(.regular); NSApplication.shared.activate(ignoringOtherApps: true) }; var body: some Scene { WindowGroup { ContentView() } } }` — include `import AppKit` at the top
WRONG (window hidden forever): `@main struct MyApp: App { var body: some Scene { WindowGroup { ContentView() } } }`

Real incident 2026-04-23: Zombie Tower Defense built + launched (PID 33983, sleeping on GUI event loop), log empty, no errors — but no window visible on desktop. Missing activation-policy calls was the cause.

guidelines.rule: The root ContentView (or equivalent root SwiftUI view) MUST declare an explicit .frame(minWidth:, minHeight:) so the window opens at a usable size. Recommended minimum 600×400; games/dashboards usually want 800×600 or larger.
guidelines.why: SwiftUI on macOS opens windows at the intrinsic content size of the root view. A VStack of text has no intrinsic size, so the window can open at 50×50 pt in the top-left corner — easy to miss, easy to mistake for 'no window opened'. Pair with .windowResizability(.contentSize) on the Scene so the user can grow the window.
examples: struct ContentView: View { var body: some View { VStack { /* UI */ }.frame(minWidth: 800, minHeight: 600) } }
Scene pattern: `WindowGroup { ContentView() }.windowResizability(.contentSize)`

guidelines.rule: Print a one-line launch confirmation to stdout from init() so the wizard's CLI runner and the user can both confirm the process reached its GUI event loop.
guidelines.why: Compiles-successfully ≠ window-appears. A stdout print from init() is a deterministic signal that the @main struct was actually instantiated (not just compiled), and gives the user visible confirmation in the terminal even while they alt-tab hunting for the window.
examples: init() { NSApplication.shared.setActivationPolicy(.regular); NSApplication.shared.activate(ignoringOtherApps: true); print("[MyApp] launched — window should be visible") }

guidelines.rule: The `@main` struct's containing target name MUST match the executableTarget name in Package.swift, AND the file containing `@main` MUST live at `Sources/<TargetName>/<anything>.swift`. Mismatches cause linker errors like `"_<TargetName>_main", referenced from: _main in command-line-aliases-file; ld: symbol(s) not found for architecture arm64`.
guidelines.why: Swift Package Manager generates `_<TargetName>_main` as the linkage symbol for executableTarget entries. If `Package.swift` declares `executableTarget(name: "ZombieTD", path: "Sources/ZombieTD")` but the actual `@main` struct is inside `Sources/Here/HereApp.swift`, or the executable target is named `Here` while the `@main` file lives in `Sources/ZombieTD/`, the linker looks for `_ZombieTD_main` and fails. This is not a Swift compile error — it's a link-time error, so auto-fix cycles that read Swift diagnostics don't catch it. Every generated macOS app must have (a) one executableTarget, (b) target name matches the `@main` struct's directory, (c) that directory contains exactly one `@main` annotation. Rename the target OR move the file — pick one and be consistent.
examples: CORRECT: Package.swift has `.executableTarget(name: "ZombieTD", path: "Sources/ZombieTD")` AND `Sources/ZombieTD/ZombieTDApp.swift` contains `@main struct ZombieTDApp: App { ... }`

WRONG (linker error): Package.swift says target name `Here` but `@main` struct lives in `Sources/ZombieTD/ZombieTDApp.swift` — linker looks for `_Here_main`, finds nothing. Fix: rename the target to `ZombieTD` in Package.swift (`name: "ZombieTD"`, `path: "Sources/ZombieTD"`) and keep the directory + filename in sync.

Rule of thumb: the directory name under `Sources/`, the `executableTarget.name` in Package.swift, and the file containing `@main` should all agree on the same concept (e.g., all say `ZombieTD` or all say `TowerDefense`). A single mismatch breaks the linker.

Real incident 2026-04-23: Zombie TD session failed repeatedly with `"_Here_main", referenced from: _main in command-line-aliases-file` because the Package target was named `Here` while the @main file was `ZombieTDApp.swift` under a `ZombieTD/` directory.

guidelines.rule: After a successful `swift build`, produce a proper `.app` bundle at `./build/<slug>-v<NNNN>.app` inside the working directory — NOT the bare executable at `.build/arm64-apple-macosx/debug/<binary>`. Use 4-digit zero-padded version numbers (v0001, v0002, …) and the kebab-case slug of the app name.
guidelines.why: mChatAI+ runs an FSEventStream watcher on the session's working folder. When it sees a `.app` appear at `./build/<slug>-v<NNNN>.app`, it auto-promotes that bundle into `MacOSAppManager` as a new numbered version and flips the session's UI to show an 'Open App' launcher. A bare executable at `.build/...` is invisible to users — they can't double-click to launch it, the window activation rules differ from a proper bundle, and the session sits in 'failed' state even though the build worked. Every macOS end-product type (mini-app, macOS app, future types) shares the `v%04d` filename scheme so directory listings sort correctly and users see a consistent naming convention.
examples:
After `swift build` succeeds, run:
```bash
mkdir -p build
NEXT=$(ls build 2>/dev/null | grep -oE 'v[0-9]{4}' | sort | tail -1 | sed 's/v//')
NEXT=$(printf 'v%04d' $((${NEXT:-0} + 1)))
SLUG=$(basename "$(pwd)" | sed 's/[^a-z0-9-]/-/g')
APP_DIR="build/${SLUG}-${NEXT}.app"
mkdir -p "$APP_DIR/Contents/MacOS"
cp .build/arm64-apple-macosx/debug/<BinaryName> "$APP_DIR/Contents/MacOS/<BinaryName>"
cat > "$APP_DIR/Contents/Info.plist" <<'EOF'
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<!DOCTYPE plist PUBLIC "-//Apple//DTD PLIST 1.0//EN" "http://www.apple.com/DTDs/PropertyList-1.0.dtd">
<plist version="1.0">
<dict>
  <key>CFBundleExecutable</key><string><BinaryName></string>
  <key>CFBundleIdentifier</key><string>com.mchatai.generated.<slug></string>
  <key>CFBundleName</key><string><DisplayName></string>
  <key>CFBundlePackageType</key><string>APPL</string>
  <key>CFBundleShortVersionString</key><string>1.0</string>
  <key>LSMinimumSystemVersion</key><string>14.0</string>
</dict>
</plist>
EOF
open "$APP_DIR"
```
Bundle path format: `build/zombie-tower-defense-v0001.app`, next build → `build/zombie-tower-defense-v0002.app`, etc.
NEVER ship just `.build/arm64-apple-macosx/debug/BinaryName` as the end-product — that's a Swift Package build artifact, not a launchable macOS app.

guidelines.rule: Use the canonical scaffold at `assets/scaffolds/macos-swiftpm-app/` as the starting structure for every macOSApp generation. Copy its Package.swift, Sources/<TargetName>/<TargetName>App.swift, ContentView.swift, build_app.sh, and Info.plist.template — substituting <TargetName>, <slug>, and <DisplayName> tokens — instead of authoring these files from scratch each time. Read the scaffold's CONTEXT.md before generating.
guidelines.why: Every macOS app session that has shipped successfully reproduces the same five files in the same shape: a Package.swift with one executableTarget, a `<TargetName>App.swift` with activation-policy + window-promotion in init(), a ContentView.swift with explicit minWidth/minHeight, a build_app.sh that wraps the binary into `build/<slug>-v<NNNN>.app`, and an Info.plist. Sessions that hand-roll these from scratch repeatedly hit the same failures: `_<TargetName>_main` linker errors (mac-005), invisible windows (mac-001), 50×50pt windows (mac-002), missing .app bundles (mac-004). The scaffold encodes every previous fix; using it is faster AND more reliable. The window-promotion `Timer.scheduledTimer` flip-to-floating-then-back trick in particular is non-obvious and hard to rediscover.
examples: First step of any macOSApp generation: `cat assets/scaffolds/macos-swiftpm-app/CONTEXT.md` (or read it via mchatai-source) to confirm the file layout and token-replacement rules.

Then copy the scaffold tree into the workDir — replacing `__TargetName__` directory + filename markers with the chosen PascalCase target name, and `<TargetName>` / `<slug>` / `<DisplayName>` string tokens inside file bodies.
Customize ContentView.swift with the actual app UI; leave Package.swift, <TargetName>App.swift's activation-policy block, and build_app.sh structurally untouched.

guidelines.rule: Every custom Notification.Name you reference (like `.newGame`, `.newMission`, `.resetTerrain`) MUST be declared in EXACTLY ONE place via `extension Notification.Name { static let newGame = Notification.Name("newGame") }`. Put this declaration in your top-level `<App>App.swift` file (alongside `@main struct App`) so all targets have access. Do NOT spread declarations across multiple files (you'll get duplicate-definition errors). Better still: AVOID NotificationCenter entirely for menu-driven actions — use SwiftUI CommandMenu Button(action:) closures that call your game-state methods directly.
guidelines.why: Recurring compile failure across wave-4 macOSApp canaries: LLM declares `NotificationCenter.default.post(name: .newGame, ...)` but never declares the corresponding `extension Notification.Name { static let newGame = ... }`. Compiler error: `type 'NSNotification.Name?' has no member 'newGame'`. Because the post site and the listener site are often in different files (App.swift posts on Cmd+N, GameScene.swift listens), the LLM's attention drops the declaration entirely. Hit by Bomberman 2026-04-30 (`.newGame`) + Lunar Lander 2026-04-30 (`.newMission` + `.resetTerrain`). Auto-fix couldn't resolve in 2 attempts because the fix requires understanding the full file map, not a local edit.
examples: BETTER (no NotificationCenter at all): `CommandMenu("Game") { Button("New Game") { gameState.newGame() }.keyboardShortcut("n") }` — direct method call on a @StateObject or @Bindable game-state singleton. NotificationCenter is overkill for menu actions in a SwiftUI app.
WRONG (compile fail): `Button("New Game") { NotificationCenter.default.post(name: .newGame, object: nil) }` without the `extension Notification.Name { static let newGame = ... }` declaration.

guidelines.rule: If your code uses `Bundle.module` (e.g. `Bundle.module.url(forResource:withExtension:)`), Package.swift's executable target MUST include `resources: [.process("Resources")]` AND a `Sources/<Target>/Resources/<file>` directory must exist with the actual file. Otherwise `Bundle.module` does NOT exist as a symbol and you get `type 'Bundle' has no member 'module'`. If you do NOT actually need bundled resources, hard-code your data inline (in a Swift `let` array/dictionary) instead of reaching for Bundle.module.
guidelines.why: Bundle.module is a synthesized symbol that SwiftPM only emits when the target's resources: clause is non-empty. The LLM frequently writes `Bundle.module.url(forResource:)` casually (it's the right pattern in PRINCIPLE) but doesn't add the matching Package.swift resources directive AND doesn't create the Resources/ directory with the actual file. Result: cryptic `'Bundle' has no member 'module'` compile error. Hit by Spelling-Bee wave-4 2026-04-30 (auto-fix recovered after adding Resources/dictionary.txt + the resources directive).
examples: REQUIRED in Package.swift: `.executableTarget(name: "MyApp", path: "Sources/MyApp", resources: [.process("Resources")])` — note BOTH the directive AND a real Resources/ dir at Sources/MyApp/Resources/ with the file you load.
REQUIRED in Sources/MyApp/Resources/dictionary.txt: actual file content (one word per line, etc.). Empty directories don't trigger SwiftPM's bundle synthesis.

BETTER (no resources at all): `private static let dictionary: Set<String> = ["RANG", "RING", ...]` inline. ~5KB of words is fine inline; don't reach for Bundle.module unless you have >50KB of asset data.

guidelines.rule: Avoid LARGE multi-line string literals (tables, dictionaries, embedded data, level layouts >500 chars) inside Swift source. If you have data that's >500 chars total, put it in `Sources/<Target>/Resources/<file>.json` and load via Bundle.module — never hard-code as a multi-line `"""raw string"""`. Multi-line raw strings spanning 200+ lines are the #1 cause of `unterminated string literal` compile errors in generator output: the LLM loses track of the closing `"""` boundary on long files OR a mid-string `\\` gets double-escaped during stream-json deserialization.
guidelines.why: Two compile-error patterns share this root cause: (a) `unterminated string literal` repeated at consecutive lines (Marble Madness wave-4 GameScene.swift line 533/534 hit this — LLM ran out of attention before closing the `"""`), (b) `'c' invalid digit` on hex-color literals adjacent to a broken raw string (the parser's recovery picks up garbage). Auto-fix typically can't repair these because the fixer rewrites larger and larger sections trying to find the boundary. Filed pattern in followup_macos_app_recurring_compile_errors.md.
examples:
WRONG (likely to break): `let levelData = """
#####
#...#
... 200 lines ...
"""`

RIGHT: put the level in `Sources/MyApp/Resources/level1.txt`, load via `let url = Bundle.module.url(forResource: "level1", withExtension: "txt")!; let data = try String(contentsOf: url, encoding: .utf8)`
RIGHT for small data (<100 lines): `private static let levels: [String] = [\"#####\", \"#...#\", ...]` — array of short strings is robust because each `"` boundary is local.

guidelines.rule: When Package.swift declares `resources: [.process("Resources")]`, you MUST create the Resources directory at `Sources/<Target>/Resources/` and place at least one file in it. SwiftPM warns `Invalid Resource 'Resources': File not found.` at build time when the directive is set but the directory is empty or absent. The build still succeeds (warning, not error) but downstream Bundle.module lookups return nil, and the harness's auto-fix loop can mis-classify this transient warning as a hard failure ("Build reported failure but no compile errors were detected"). If you don't need a Resources/ dir, omit the `resources:` parameter entirely.
guidelines.why: Filed in followup_macos_app_recurring_compile_errors.md from Spelling-Bee wave-4 sequence. Belt-and-suspenders rule paired with mac-008.
examples: REQUIRED: `Sources/MyApp/Resources/.gitkeep` (or any file) AND `resources: [.process("Resources")]` in Package.swift — both or neither.
WRONG: `resources: [.process("Resources")]` in Package.swift but NO `Sources/MyApp/Resources/` directory on disk. SwiftPM warns + Bundle.module returns nil at runtime.

guidelines.rule: When Package.swift's executableTarget uses `path: "Sources/<TargetName>"` (nested), EVERY .swift file in your project MUST live at `Sources/<TargetName>/*.swift` — NEVER at `Sources/*.swift` (one level shallower). SwiftPM compiles files only at the configured target path; @main files at the wrong depth get IGNORED during compile but the linker still searches for `_<TargetName>_main` symbol → 'Undefined symbols for architecture arm64' link error. Pick ONE layout per project: either flat (`path: "Sources"` + files at `Sources/*.swift`) or nested (`path: "Sources/<Target>"` + files at `Sources/<Target>/*.swift`). Don't mix.
guidelines.why: Hit by Wave-4 round-3 Adventure-reboot 2026-04-30: LLM emitted both `Sources/AtariApp.swift` (@main = AdventureRebootApp) AND `Sources/AdventureReboot/AdventureRebootApp.swift` (@main = AdventureRebootApp duplicate). Package.swift had `path: "Sources/AdventureReboot"` so only the nested files compiled. The orphan AtariApp.swift at the shallow depth got ignored by SwiftPM but its @main was the one the auto-fix loop kept regenerating. Result: 'Undefined symbols ... _AdventureReboot_main' link error that the auto-fix loop can never repair (it's a topology bug, not a code bug). Same family hit Bomberman wave-4-round-2 (`@main` file at `Sources/BombermanApp.swift` but target path was `Sources/Bomberman/`).
examples: RIGHT (flat layout): Package.swift `.executableTarget(name: "MyApp", path: "Sources")` + ALL files at `Sources/*.swift` (no subdirectory).
RIGHT (nested layout): Package.swift `.executableTarget(name: "MyApp", path: "Sources/MyApp")` + ALL files at `Sources/MyApp/*.swift` including the @main App struct file.
WRONG: target `path: "Sources/MyApp"` but @main file at `Sources/MyAppApp.swift` (one level shallower). Linker error.
WRONG: target `path: "Sources"` but you create a `Sources/MyApp/` subdirectory and put files there. Linker error (target compiles 0 files).

guidelines.rule: GameplayKit API: use `GKGoal(toAvoid: <obstacles>, maxPredictionTime: <s>)` — NOT `GKGoal(toAvoidObstacles: ..., maxPredictionTime: ...)`. The `toAvoidObstacles:` initializer was renamed to `toAvoid:` in modern SDKs (macOS 12+). Same renaming applied to `GKGoal(toFleeAgent:)`, `GKGoal(toSeekAgent:)`, etc. — those are the current names; the older `toFleeFromAgent:` / `toSeekTargetAgent:` form gives an 'has been renamed' error.
guidelines.why: Hit by Wave-4 round-3 Coleco Football 2026-04-30: GameScene.swift:330 used `GKGoal(toAvoidObstacles: obstacles, maxPredictionTime: 1.5)`. Compiler error: 'init(toAvoidObstacles:maxPredictionTime:) has been renamed to init(toAvoid:maxPredictionTime:)'. Auto-fix can repair this in one edit but burns a turn + masks the underlying API knowledge gap. Spec the canonical form in wisdom so the LLM emits it on first try.
examples: RIGHT: `let goal = GKGoal(toAvoid: [obstacle], maxPredictionTime: 1.5)`
RIGHT: `let goal = GKGoal(toFleeAgent: pacmanAgent)` (NOT toFleeFromAgent)
RIGHT: `let goal = GKGoal(toSeekAgent: ballCarrierAgent)` (NOT toSeekTargetAgent)
WRONG: `let goal = GKGoal(toAvoidObstacles: [obstacle], maxPredictionTime: 1.5)` — compiler error 'has been renamed to init(toAvoid:maxPredictionTime:)'.

guidelines.rule: NEVER `import SwiftUICore` — that is a PRIVATE Apple framework, not part of the public SDK. Linker rejects it with 'cannot link directly with SwiftUICore because product being built is not an allowed client of it'. If you reach for SwiftUICore for some advanced internal type, find the public SwiftUI alternative or simply use what's in `import SwiftUI` + `import AppKit`. Same applies to other underscored / Core-suffixed private frameworks (`SwiftUI/_SwiftUI_AppKit`, `_SwiftUIShims`, etc.) — never import them directly.
guidelines.why: Hit by Wave-4 round-3 Adventure-reboot 2026-04-30: link error 'Could not parse or use implicit file SwiftUICore.tbd: cannot link directly with SwiftUICore because product being built is not an allowed client of it'. The LLM probably reached for it after seeing it in some StackOverflow answer; the public SwiftUI surface always covers what's needed for an app target.
examples: RIGHT: `import SwiftUI` + `import AppKit`
WRONG: `import SwiftUICore` — link error, will not build.
WRONG: `@_implementationOnly import SwiftUICore` — same error. Underscored imports of Apple internals are rejected at link time even if the compile passes.

guidelines.rule: SpriteKit games MUST wire all game-event callbacks in the `update(_ currentTime:)` loop or via `didBeginContact` — do NOT write standalone score/collect/eat/collision functions without calling them. Every class that 'owns' a game-event function MUST also be the place it is called. Common missing wires: (1) Pac-Man / collect-dot: `GameScene.update` must call `if scene.tiles[tile.row][tile.col] == .dot { scene.dotNodes[tile.row][tile.col]?.removeFromParent(); scene.gameState.eatDot() }` after `pacMan.update`. (2) Ghost-player collision: after `ghost.update`, check `distanceSq(ghost.position, pacMan.position) < collisionRadius*collisionRadius`. (3) Power-pellet: same check against pellet node positions. (4) Ghost-eat: check if ghost is frightened AND close to Pac-Man. If you generate a `GameState.eatDot()` method but never call it from the update loop, the score never changes and dots never disappear.
guidelines.why: Wave-4 round-3 macOS Pac-Man shipped with correct GameState methods (eatDot, eatPowerPellet, eatGhost) and correct dot/ghost node structures, but PacManNode.update() had zero calls back to GameState and zero dot-removal code. Result: dots never disappeared, ghosts were never triggered. The LLM correctly wrote the individual building blocks but missed the glue. Rule filed 2026-05-01.
examples: RIGHT: In GameScene.update after pacMan.update(deltaTime:in:self): check tile at pacMan's current grid position, if .dot: remove dotNode, call gameState.eatDot(), set tiles[row][col] = .empty
WRONG: Having a `gameState.eatDot()` function that is never called from anywhere in update()

RIGHT: In GameScene.update after ghost.update: `if dist(ghost.position, pacMan.position) < threshold { if ghost.isFrightened { ghost.isEaten = true; gameState.eatGhost() } else { handlePacManCaught() } }`
WRONG: Writing handlePacManCaught() as a standalone method that requires an external caller but having no caller in the update loop

guidelines.rule: Any `static` method that touches an `@MainActor` (or `@Published`) property — e.g. on an `ObservableObject` `GameState` — MUST itself be annotated `@MainActor`. Otherwise Swift 6 strict concurrency rejects with `main actor-isolated property 'X' can not be referenced from a nonisolated context`. The two most common offenders: (a) `static func draw(in:size:cellSize:game:time:)` on a renderer enum/struct that reads `game.popup`, `game.pacDirection`, etc. — annotate the whole `static func draw(...)` with `@MainActor`. (b) `static func printGameSmoke()` on a smoke-test enum that constructs a `GameState()` and reads its published vars — same fix. The compiler note even spells the fix: `add '@MainActor' to make static method 'draw(in:size:cellSize:game:time:)' part of global actor 'MainActor'`.
guidelines.why: macOS Pac-Man canary 2026-05-03 hit this on TWO files (MazeRenderer.swift static draw + GameState.swift PacManSmokeTests.printGameSmoke) within one generation. The wizard's auto-fix loop didn't squash both within maxTurns=4. The fix is mechanical (one annotation) but the LLM forgets it on first emission whenever the renderer/smoke-test is split out as `enum SomeStatic { static func ... }`. Adding `@MainActor` at the static-function level is cheaper than turning the whole enum into `@MainActor` because most static helpers are pure.
examples: RIGHT: `enum MazeRenderer { @MainActor static func draw(in ctx: inout GraphicsContext, size: CGSize, cellSize: Double, game: GameState, time: TimeInterval) { ... } }`
RIGHT: `enum PacManSmokeTests { @MainActor static func printGameSmoke() { let game = GameState(); print(game.pacDirection.rawValue) } }`
WRONG: `enum MazeRenderer { static func draw(..., game: GameState, ...) { _ = game.popup } }` — compile error: 'main actor-isolated property popup can not be referenced from a nonisolated context'.

Also accept: `@MainActor enum MazeRenderer { ... }` annotates every member at once. Useful when MOST static methods touch the actor — but adds isolation to pure helpers too, which makes them harder to call from non-main contexts. Prefer per-method when in doubt.

guidelines.rule: A `guard <condition> else { ... }` body MUST end with a control-flow exit on EVERY path: `return`, `throw`, `continue`, `break`, `fatalError(...)`, `precondition(false)`. Specifically, do NOT write `guard X else { if Y { setupY() } else { return } }` — the if-branch falls through and the compiler rejects: `'guard' body must not fall through, consider using a 'return' or 'throw' to exit the scope`. If you need 'set up Y, then continue' as the recovery branch, refactor to a plain `if X == .empty { ... }` block (no guard) — guards are for early-exit only. Pattern that bit Pac-Man movePac(): `guard pacDirection != .none else { if pacQueued != .none, isPassable(...) { pacDirection = pacQueued; pacQueued = .none } else { return } }` — the if-branch's intent is 'recover and proceed' but guard syntax forbids that. Replace with: `if pacDirection == .none { if pacQueued != .none, isPassable(...) { pacDirection = pacQueued; pacQueued = .none } else { return } }`.
guidelines.why: Pac-Man canary 2026-05-03 emitted the bad guard pattern in `movePac(dt:)`. The auto-fix loop didn't catch it. Filed so future macOSApp generators recognize 'I want guard for early-exit but I also want a recovery branch' and reach for plain if/else instead.
examples: RIGHT (recovery + continue): `if pacDirection == .none { if pacQueued != .none, canMove(pacQueued) { pacDirection = pacQueued; pacQueued = .none } else { return } }; advance(...)`
RIGHT (pure early-exit, no recovery): `guard pacDirection != .none else { return }; advance(...)`
WRONG: `guard pacDirection != .none else { if pacQueued != .none, canMove(pacQueued) { pacDirection = pacQueued } else { return } }` — compile error: 'guard body must not fall through'.

guidelines.rule: `SKPhysicsBody(ellipseOf:)` DOES NOT EXIST — but `SKShapeNode(ellipseOf:)` DOES. Don't confuse them. SpriteKit's SKPhysicsBody public initializers are: `init(circleOfRadius:)`, `init(circleOfRadius:center:)`, `init(rectangleOf:)`, `init(rectangleOf:center:)`, `init(polygonFrom:)` (CGPath), `init(edgeChainFrom:)`, `init(edgeFrom:to:)`, `init(edgeLoopFrom:)`, `init(texture:size:)`, `init(texture:alphaThreshold:size:)`, `init(bodies:)`. There is NO ellipse/oval initializer ON SKPhysicsBody. SKShapeNode by contrast DOES expose `init(ellipseOf:)`, `init(ellipseIn:)` for visual oval shapes — those are correct and you should keep them. The compiler reports: `SKPhysicsBody: incorrect argument label in call (have 'ellipseOf:', expected 'rectangleOf:')` — Xcode's fixit suggests rectangleOf which is usually what you want for the physics body. If you want an oval physics body shape, use `SKPhysicsBody(polygonFrom:)` with a CGPath of an ellipse — but for almost all game characters (knights, ostriches, ghosts, etc.) `circleOfRadius:` or `rectangleOf:` is correct and cheaper.
guidelines.why: Wave-4 round-6 Joust canary 2026-05-04 hit this in MountNode.swift: `SKPhysicsBody(ellipseOf: CGSize(width:38,height:30))`. The LLM hallucinated the `ellipseOf:` initializer — probably from Core Graphics' `NSBezierPath(ovalIn:)` or `CGPath.addEllipse(in:)` which DO exist. SpriteKit's body API is more limited. Auto-fix attempts misread the fixit and tried 'rectangleOf' which works. Adding to wisdom so the LLM emits the right form on first try.
examples: RIGHT (round body — knights, ghosts, marbles): `let body = SKPhysicsBody(circleOfRadius: 19)`
RIGHT (rectangular body — bricks, platforms, ships): `let body = SKPhysicsBody(rectangleOf: CGSize(width: 38, height: 30))`
RIGHT (custom shape — ostrich+knight outline): `let path = CGMutablePath(); path.addEllipse(in: CGRect(x: -19, y: -15, width: 38, height: 30)); let body = SKPhysicsBody(polygonFrom: path)`
WRONG: `SKPhysicsBody(ellipseOf: CGSize(width:38,height:30))` — initializer doesn't exist; compiler gives 'incorrect argument label, expected rectangleOf:'.

guidelines.rule: When a class has implicitly-unwrapped optional (IUO) property like `var p2Mount: MountNode!`, a ternary or if-expression that returns it OR another non-optional value (e.g. `let node = (player == .one) ? p1Mount : p2Mount`) infers `MountNode?` (NOT `MountNode!` and NOT `MountNode`), because Swift type inference unifies branches as Optional in expressions. Pass-through to a non-optional argument fails: `value of optional type 'MountNode?' must be unwrapped to a value of type 'MountNode'`. Fix: explicitly annotate the binding's type — `let node: MountNode = (player == .one) ? p1Mount : p2Mount` — which lets Swift force-unwrap the IUO at the call site. Alternative: force-unwrap with `!` at usage. AVOID making both branches return Optional and then unwrapping later — that's noisier and risks crashes if either branch becomes nil.
guidelines.why: Wave-4 round-6 Joust canary 2026-05-04 hit this in JoustGameScene.respawnMount: `let node = (player == .one) ? p1Mount : p2Mount` — both p1Mount and p2Mount are `MountNode!` IUOs but Swift inferred `node: MountNode?`. Two compile errors at addChild(node) and placeMount(node, ...). Auto-fix didn't recognize the IUO/ternary interaction (a Swift idiom most LLMs miss). Add explicit type annotation as the canonical fix.
examples: RIGHT: `let node: MountNode = (player == .one) ? p1Mount : p2Mount; addChild(node)` — explicit type forces IUO unwrap.
RIGHT (force-unwrap inline): `addChild((player == .one) ? p1Mount! : p2Mount!)` — verbose but works.
WRONG: `let node = (player == .one) ? p1Mount : p2Mount; addChild(node)` — node inferred as MountNode?; addChild fails.
Also applies to: `if/else` returning IUOs, `switch` returning IUOs — same Swift inference rule. Always annotate the binding type when mixing IUOs in branching expressions.

guidelines.rule:
Multi-line text inside `String(format: "...")`, `print("...")`, or any double-quoted Swift string literal MUST escape newlines as `
` — DO NOT put a literal newline inside `"..."`. The compiler reports `unterminated string literal`. If you genuinely want multi-line text without escapes, use triple-quoted Swift literals: `""" line1
 line2 """` (the indentation rules are particular — see Swift's multiline string spec). For `String(format:)` HUD formatting, ALWAYS use `
` between lines. Real example that bit Lunar Lander 2026-05-04: `hudLabel.text = String(format: "ALT %5.0f<newline>VVEL %+6.2f", alt, vy)` — the compiler hits the literal newline and sees an unterminated string.
guidelines.why: Wave-4 round-6 Lunar Lander L3 canary 2026-05-04: 16 'unterminated string literal' errors at GameScene.swift lines 315-321 because the LLM wrote multi-line `String(format:)` calls with literal newlines instead of `\n`. Auto-fix didn't resolve. The LLM probably came from Python or another language where literal newlines in strings are fine; Swift doesn't allow that in double-quoted literals.
examples: RIGHT: `String(format: "ALT %5.0f\nVVEL %+6.2f", alt, vy)` — escaped newline.
RIGHT (multi-line, when really wanting multiple lines): `""" ALT \(alt)\n VVEL \(vy) """`
WRONG: `String(format: "ALT %5.0f<actual newline>VVEL %+6.2f", ...)` — compile error 'unterminated string literal'.

guidelines.rule: Swift 6 strict concurrency: `SKPhysicsContactDelegate.didBegin(_ contact:)` is `nonisolated` by default, but the typical handler body needs to call `@MainActor`-isolated game state. The contact parameter is task-isolated and cannot be sent into a `Task { @MainActor in }` closure (`error: sending 'contact' risks causing data races`). Fix: capture the SENDABLE data from the contact (node references, categoryBitMask values, position values) BEFORE entering the Task closure. SKPhysicsBody's `node` and `categoryBitMask` are sendable; SKPhysicsContact itself is not. Pattern: `nonisolated func didBegin(_ contact: SKPhysicsContact) { let nodeA = contact.bodyA.node; let nodeB = contact.bodyB.node; let cats = (contact.bodyA.categoryBitMask, contact.bodyB.categoryBitMask); Task { @MainActor in ... use nodeA/nodeB/cats ... } }`.
guidelines.why: Wave-4 round-6 Joust canary 2026-05-04 hit this. The auto-fix attempts repeatedly captured `contact` itself into the @MainActor Task closure, which Swift 6 forbids. Capturing only the sendable fields BEFORE the closure unblocks the build. SKNode is @MainActor-isolated for most properties but the reference itself is sendable; same for category bitmasks (UInt32). This pattern works for any nonisolated SKPhysicsContactDelegate or SKSceneDelegate callback that needs to bounce to main actor.
examples: RIGHT: ```nonisolated func didBegin(_ contact: SKPhysicsContact) { let nodeA = contact.bodyA.node; let nodeB = contact.bodyB.node; let cats = (contact.bodyA.categoryBitMask, contact.bodyB.categoryBitMask); Task { @MainActor in if cats.0 == Cat.lava, let mn = nodeB as? MountNode { await self.killMount(mn.player) } } }```
WRONG: ```nonisolated func didBegin(_ contact: SKPhysicsContact) { Task { @MainActor in let nodeA = contact.bodyA.node /* error: sending 'contact' risks data races */ } }```
Also: `didEnd(_:)`, `update(_:)`, and any other SKScene/SKPhysicsContactDelegate callback follows the same rule — capture sendable fields before the @MainActor hop.
Alternative: mark the whole class `@MainActor` (drop the `nonisolated`). Works for small games but loses parallelism; pre-capture is cleaner.

guidelines.rule: Card-game UI MUST use PlayingCardSuit.glyph (Unicode suit chars) and PlayingCardSuit.rgb to render cards — NOT first-letter substring like 'C' / 'D' / 'H' / 'S'. The catalog component cardgame.core exposes both `glyph: String` and `rgb: (Double, Double, Double)` for exactly this reason. 'C' and 'D' are ambiguous (Card? Diamond?) and look amateurish. Same applies to other unicode-rich domains: chess pieces (use the chess unicode block), checkers (filled vs hollow circles), playing dice, etc. — when the catalog component provides a `glyph` accessor, use it.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-04 on shipped Native Blackjack: "the cards use C for clubs and D for diamonds". The LLM picked PlayingCardSuit.rawValue.first because there was no canonical display API on the suit enum. Adding `glyph` to PlayingCardSuit + this wisdom rule together prevent the regression.
examples: RIGHT: `Text(card.suit.glyph)` (renders Unicode suit char)
WRONG: `Text(String(card.suit.rawValue.prefix(1).uppercased()))` (renders 'C' for clubs — ambiguous + unprofessional)
RIGHT (color): `let c = card.suit.rgb; Color(red: c.red, green: c.green, blue: c.blue)` — diamonds/hearts get red, clubs/spades get black.

Note: PlayingCardSuit.glyph + .rgb were added to cardgame.core 2026-05-04. If your generated app says these accessors don't exist, you're using a stale catalog snapshot — refresh via tunnel refreshMchataisourceCache.

guidelines.rule: Every game MUST present a VISIBLE on-screen restart/play-again button when the game ends (game-over, you-win, level-complete). Do NOT rely solely on a menu shortcut like Cmd+N or 'Game > New Game' — users don't discover those mid-play. The restart UI must: (a) appear automatically when the terminal phase is reached, (b) be visually distinct (colored button or large tappable area), (c) clearly say 'Restart' / 'Play Again' / 'New Game' (not just 'OK' or unlabeled), (d) when tapped, fully reset all game state (score, lives, level, score-multiplier, fuel, terrain — anything that accumulated during play). Keep the menu-bar shortcut too (e.g. Cmd+N), but the on-screen button is the primary affordance.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-04 on shipped Lunar Lander: 'There is no restart button for the game: That should be a fundamental wisdom rule to all games if we don't have that already.' The shipped Lunar Lander rendered 'GAME OVER score=0' as static HUD text with no UI affordance. Users had to discover Cmd+N from the menu bar. Same pattern was hit by earlier shipped Pong, BurgerTime, Joust — they all have the menu shortcut but lack a tappable on-screen restart. This is the single most common 'it's a great game but UX feels rough' complaint.
examples: RIGHT (SwiftUI overlay over SpriteKit scene): `if game.phase == .gameOver { VStack { Text('GAME OVER').font(.largeTitle); Text('Score: \(game.score)'); Button('Play Again') { game.requestNewMission() }.keyboardShortcut(.return).buttonStyle(.borderedProminent).controlSize(.large) }.padding(40).background(.ultraThinMaterial).cornerRadius(20) }
RIGHT (SpriteKit-only): on phase change, addChild an SKShapeNode container with SKLabelNode children, set userInteractionEnabled, handle mouseDown to call scene.startNewMission(resetTerrain: true).
RIGHT pair pattern: keep the CommandMenu 'New Game' Cmd+N as a redundant shortcut, but the on-screen button is the primary affordance.
WRONG: Setting `game.status = 'GAME OVER score=0'` in the HUD label and stopping the game. User can't restart without using the menu bar.
WRONG: Only the menu-bar shortcut. macOS users don't discover Cmd+N mid-play unless explicitly told.
Real incident 2026-05-04: shipped Lunar Lander had 'GAME OVER score=0' as a static HUD label with no UI button. User reported needing a restart button as fundamental wisdom for all games.

guidelines.rule: Do NOT prefix the user-facing app title with 'Native'. e.g. write 'Mastermind', 'Asteroids', 'Hold'em Poker' — NOT 'Native Mastermind', 'Native Asteroids', 'Native Hold'em Poker'. The word 'Native' is mChatAI's internal-architecture label distinguishing macos-components-built apps from web-mini-apps. End users don't care about that distinction; the prefix just makes every title noisy + samey. The ID/slug can keep 'native-' as a namespace (e.g. recipe ID `recipe.native-word-game` is fine for catalog routing) but the rendered Text("...") in the title bar / hero label / WindowGroup name MUST be the bare product name.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-04 after reviewing the catalog reference apps: "The 'Native' word is everywhere in here, it's a bit overkill". Stripping it across 9 example ContentViews fixed the immediate visual noise, but every NEW catalog example generation could re-introduce it. Locking the rule in wisdom prevents recurrence.
examples: RIGHT: Text("Mastermind"), WindowGroup("Mini Crossword"), Text("Hold'em Poker")
WRONG: Text("Native Mastermind"), WindowGroup("Native Mini Crossword"), Text("Native Hold'em Poker")
OK in identifiers (not user-facing): recipe IDs, target names, bundle identifiers like com.mchatai.wizard.native-poker — those stay namespaced.
Real edits: 9 examples in mchatai-source/frameworks/macos-components/examples/ all said Text("Native X") in their hero labels. Stripped 2026-05-04 (commit pending).

guidelines.rule: When you highlight a selected/active cell in a grid game (crossword, sudoku, minesweeper, board game), pick the cell color and the text color as a PAIR. Either (a) light pale-blue cell with dark/black glyph, or (b) saturated blue cell with white glyph — but NOT a saturated dark blue cell that the user is supposed to type letters into while the glyph is unreadable. Lawrence's standing preference for puzzle games: lighter palettes (pale-blue selection at ~Color(red:0.85, green:0.92, blue:1.0), very-pale-blue active row/col at ~Color(red:0.93, green:0.96, blue:1.0)) with dark text. The 'fully saturated .blue with .white text' pattern reads like a banner notification, not a typing target.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-04 on the catalog Mini Crossword: "verification, lighter colors". The reference had `cellFill: .blue` for the selected cell + `.white` text — sharp and readable but visually overwhelming for a puzzle that's mostly about reading clues + typing letters. Switched to pale-blue with dark text 2026-05-04 (commit pending).
examples: RIGHT (light palette, dark glyphs): selected = Color(red:0.85, green:0.92, blue:1.0) with .black text
RIGHT (saturated palette, light glyphs): selected = .blue with .white text — sharp but stays consistent
WRONG (mixed): selected = .blue cell + .white text where active-row cells are pale-blue + .black text — the eye does extra work to map active vs selected
Active-row/col / 'soft highlight' should be a 2x lighter version of the selected color so they read as a subtle hint, not a competing call-to-action.

guidelines.rule: The 'visible on-screen restart button' rule (mac-022 / ag-restart-button) applies to EVERY terminal phase, not just the obvious 'lose' state. Specifically: (a) win — show 'Play Again' overlay (b) lose — show 'Play Again' overlay (c) draw / stalemate (in 2-player games) — show 'Rematch' overlay (d) puzzle-solved — show 'Next puzzle' or 'Play Again' overlay. If the engine has any phase OTHER than the live 'playing' state, that phase needs a restart UI affordance, not just a status label. Keyboard shortcuts (Cmd+N, R) are an escape hatch but the on-screen button is mandatory.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-04 on the catalog Minesweeper reference: "A restart button would be nice (instead of just hitting R)". The reference had a 'New Game' button hidden in a controls row + an 'R' keyboard shortcut, but no overlay on the win/lose screen. Same pattern slipped through on the original mac-022 review. Extending the rule explicitly so future reviewers know: every non-playing phase needs an overlay button.
examples: RIGHT: `if engine.state == .won || engine.state == .lost { gameOverOverlay }` with the overlay containing a Button('Play Again') keyboardShortcut(.return) and a status message.
RIGHT (puzzle): when isSolved becomes true, overlay 'New Puzzle' button. NOT 'press Cmd+N' — the button is the affordance.
WRONG: only setting `statusMessage = 'GAME OVER'` and relying on the user to find the small 'New Game' button in a controls row.
Real incident 2026-05-04: shipped Lunar Lander v1 had 'GAME OVER score=0' as static HUD. Catalog Minesweeper reference had hidden 'New Game' in controls. Same family of misses.

guidelines.rule: Game backgrounds MUST be warm/atmospheric — radial-gradient or layered linear-gradient with at least one color highlight per corner. NOT pure black, NOT plain solid color. The web Candy Match reference uses a layered gradient: base purple (#1f1026) + radial yellow glow at top-left + radial cyan glow at top-right. Same pattern works for any game: pick a primary color matching the game's theme (purple for candy, dark blue for space, dark green for forest), add 1-2 radial corner glows in complementary colors. SwiftUI: use ZStack { Color + RadialGradient + RadialGradient }.ignoresSafeArea(). The warmth makes the game feel like a finished product instead of a wireframe.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-04 on the catalog Match3: 'The black background is lame'. Switched to layered radial-gradient (purple base + yellow + cyan corners) per the web Candy Match reference. The atmosphere is the difference between a play-test prototype and a polished app — and it costs ~12 lines of SwiftUI to add. Apply broadly: arcade games, board games, card games, puzzles all benefit.
examples: RIGHT (SwiftUI): ZStack { Color(red: 0.12, green: 0.06, blue: 0.15).ignoresSafeArea(); RadialGradient(gradient: Gradient(colors: [Color.yellow.opacity(0.22), .clear]), center: UnitPoint(x: 0.2, y: 0.1), startRadius: 0, endRadius: 360); RadialGradient(...) }

RIGHT (HTML/CSS): background: radial-gradient(circle at 20% 10%, rgba(250,204,21,0.22), transparent 28rem), radial-gradient(circle at 80% 0%, rgba(34,211,238,0.16), transparent 26rem), linear-gradient(145deg, #170b1c 0%, #1f1026 48%, #35141d 100%);
WRONG: backgroundColor = .black, .ignoresSafeArea(), nothing else. Reads like a debug build.
Board game color picks: chess = warm beige + dark walnut accents; checkers = forest green felt + tan corner glow; poker = dark green felt + brass corner highlights.

guidelines.rule: Every clickable/interactive tile in a game (candy in match-3, card in solitaire, key on a piano keyboard, etc.) MUST have: (a) a vertical or 145° linear-gradient fill, NOT a flat color. (b) An inner highlight — a rotated semi-transparent white blob or arc, inset ~12% from the edges, that gives the tile a 3D / glossy appearance. (c) A subtle 1.5pt white-with-low-opacity stroke for definition. (d) A drop-shadow whose color is the tile's BOTTOM gradient color so the tile feels grounded. The web Candy Match reference does this in CSS with `.tile::before { inset: 12%; background: rgba(255,255,255,0.22); transform: rotate(18deg) }`. SwiftUI equivalent: ZStack { RoundedRectangle.fill(LinearGradient(top, bottom)); RoundedRectangle.fill(.white.opacity(0.22)).scaleEffect(0.62).rotationEffect(.degrees(-18)); RoundedRectangle.stroke(.white.opacity(0.28), lineWidth: 1.5) }.shadow(color: bottomColor.opacity(0.5), radius: 6, y: 4).
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-04 on the catalog Match3: 'The jewels look kind of lame, the graphics are very flat and boring.' Adding the gradient + inner shine + colored shadow makes a flat shape look like a candy-shop jewel without bundling any art assets. Same trick works for cards (gradient front + back), keys (white-to-cream gradient + shine), pieces (color-toned gradient + shadow).
examples: RIGHT (Match3 candy): see /Users/lawrencenorman/mystuff/src/mchatai_platform/mchatai-source/frameworks/macos-components/examples/match3/ContentView.swift (function candyTileBody) — gradient + inner shine + stroke + colored shadow
RIGHT (web Candy Match): see frameworks/web-components/examples/candy-match/candy-match.css — .tile + .tile::before + .tile.kind-N gradient classes
WRONG: RoundedRectangle.fill(Color.red) + Image(systemName: 'sparkle') with no gradient, no shine, no shadow — reads as a wireframe placeholder
Reskin guidance: when the user asks for a different theme (e.g. 'space-themed match-3'), keep the gradient+shine+stroke STRUCTURE, only swap the gradient color pairs.

guidelines.rule: Every game state transition that the player triggers MUST have a visible animation, not a snap-cut. Specifically: (1) Tile/piece moves: spring-animation (response: 0.25-0.35, dampingFraction: 0.7) with offset interpolation. (2) Tile-swap (match-3, sliding puzzles): both tiles slide simultaneously toward each other, then settle. (3) Tile/piece removal: scale to 1.3× + fade-out (.easeOut, 0.18s). (4) Refill / collapse (match-3, tetris): new tiles slide DOWN from above using SwiftUI .transition(.asymmetric(insertion: .move(edge: .top).combined(with: .opacity), removal: .opacity)). (5) Cascade chain: stagger animations by 0.20-0.25s per chain pass so the player can see each step. (6) Score increment: animate with .contentTransition(.numericText()) or a brief scale-pulse on the score number. NEVER use snap-cuts on user-initiated state changes — even 0.1s of motion is the difference between 'game' and 'spreadsheet'.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-04 on the catalog Match3: 'the animations are still pretty lame, the jewels don't slide down, there is no pulsing hints, nothing.' Borrowing the web Candy Match's animation choreography (swap glide + pop+fade match + slide-from-top refill) made the game feel alive instead of teleporty. Apply broadly: chess piece moves should glide, card flips should rotate, hop-games should arc, everything you click should respond visibly.
examples: RIGHT (swap glide): withAnimation(.easeInOut(duration: 0.18)) { tileOffsets[a] = ...; tileOffsets[b] = ... }; DispatchQueue.main.asyncAfter(deadline: .now() + 0.20) { engine.swap(a, b); tileOffsets[a] = .zero; tileOffsets[b] = .zero }
RIGHT (pop fade): withAnimation(.easeOut(duration: 0.20)) { poppingTiles.formUnion(matched); ... } -> tile body uses .scaleEffect(isPopping ? 1.3 : 1.0).opacity(isPopping ? 0 : 1)
RIGHT (cascade stagger): for (i, event) in events.enumerated() { DispatchQueue.main.asyncAfter(deadline: .now() + Double(i) * 0.24) { ... } }
WRONG: engine.swap(a, b); engine.findMatches(); engine.collapse(); engine.refill() — all in one synchronous frame; the user sees the board jump from old state to new state with zero feedback.
Anti-pattern: rendering NEW tiles in their final position. They should slide IN from off-screen / above. Use transitions: .move(edge: .top).combined(with: .opacity).

guidelines.rule: After ~5 seconds of player idleness in a game with valid-move gameplay (match-3, sudoku, mahjong-tile-matching, etc.), the game MUST surface a hint by visibly pulsing one valid move. The hint should: (a) compute a real valid move via the engine's findPossibleSwap / findPossibleMove API, (b) animate the involved tiles with a soft scale (1.05x) + glow pulse on a 1s ease-in-out cycle, (c) cancel the hint as soon as the player makes any move. This is the difference between 'I'm stuck' and 'this game wants me to figure it out alone.' Cap idle threshold at 5-8s — any longer feels like punishment.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-04 on the catalog Match3: 'no pulsing hints, nothing'. The web Candy Match doesn't have this either, but it's a UX standard for tile-swap puzzlers. Added to Match3 with a Timer.publish(every: 1) ticker + lastMoveAt tracking + findPossibleSwap helper. Same pattern works for Mahjong (highlight a matchable pair), Sudoku (highlight a cell with only one valid digit), Solitaire (highlight a moveable card).
examples: RIGHT (idle threshold + scale-pulse): @State var lastMoveAt = Date(); Timer.publish(every: 1) { if Date.now - lastMoveAt > 5 { hintPair = engine.findPossibleSwap(); withAnimation(...) { hintPulse.toggle() } } }
RIGHT (cancel on move): when the player commits any swap/move, set hintPair = nil; lastMoveAt = Date()
WRONG: showing the hint immediately after every move (turns it into a tutorial mode the player can't disable)
WRONG: never showing a hint (advanced players don't need it, but new players quit when they're stuck and don't realize valid moves exist)

guidelines.rule: Tile-based SwiftUI games (match-3, sliding puzzles, Tetris, Bejeweled, Threes, Dominoes, Mahjong tile clears) MUST iterate tiles BY a stable id (UUID), NOT by cell position. The model `struct Tile: Identifiable { var id = UUID() }` survives moves through the grid (collapses, swaps, falls) so when SwiftUI sees the SAME id at a new position, it interpolates the position transition automatically — the tile visibly slides from its old cell to its new cell. Iterating by `grid.allPoints()` (cell positions) defeats this: SwiftUI sees "old contents gone, new contents arrived" and snap-cuts. The user reports it as 'animations are lacking' or 'tiles teleport' — symptom of a stable-identity miss, not an animation tuning issue.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-04 on the catalog Match3 v2: 'the jewels don't slide down, the animation is just one image that shows up and goes away, its not real animation' — even though v2 had withAnimation + spring + transitions. Root cause: the View iterated `ForEach(engine.grid.allPoints(), id: \.self)` so cells were the identity, not tiles. When the engine collapsed a column, the same Match3Tile UUID landed at a new cell but SwiftUI couldn't tell because it tracked cells. v3 changed to `ForEach(tilesWithPositions(), id: \.tile.id)` and the slide animation worked correctly without any extra animation code. Universal architectural rule.
examples: RIGHT: `struct GameTile: Identifiable, Equatable { var id = UUID(); var symbol: String }` + `ForEach(engine.tilesWithPositions(), id: \.tile.id) { entry in tileView(entry.tile).position(positionFor(entry.point)) }` + `.animation(.spring(...), value: engine.grid)` — SwiftUI animates each tile's position transition.

RIGHT pattern for the model: when the engine collapses, PRESERVE existing tile structs (push them onto a stack, pop back into new positions) — only create new tile structs (with new UUIDs) when REFILLING empty cells from above.

WRONG: `ForEach(engine.grid.allPoints(), id: \.self) { point in tileView(at: point) }` — cells are the identity. When tile content changes at a cell, SwiftUI redraws in place; when cells reshuffle, no animation possible because there's no continuity.
WRONG: trying to fix bad animation by adding more `.animation()` modifiers, longer durations, or `.transition()`s — none of those help if SwiftUI can't tell which old element is the new element.

Real incident 2026-05-04: catalog Match3 v2 had every animation primitive correct (spring, withAnimation, transition: .move(edge: .top), staggered DispatchQueue) but felt teleporty. Switching to tile-id iteration (commit d4a15c1) fixed the slide-down without changing any animation code — SwiftUI did the work once it had stable identity.

Bonus: this is the SwiftUI equivalent of React's `key` prop. The principle is identical: stable identity → smooth transitions. The key insight to take to other tile-based games is that the MODEL needs the ID, not the View.

guidelines.rule: When the user is validating an iteratively-rebuilt .app bundle (e.g. /tmp/catalog-builds/<recipe>/build/<App>.app or any path-stable .app you keep overwriting), `open <path>` will ONLY launch the new binary if no existing process is still running with that bundle path. macOS treats `open` as 'bring forward if already open' — it does NOT respawn a new process when one already exists for the bundle. So the user sees the OLD UI no matter how many times you rebuild + cache-sync. The clean validation loop is `pkill -f "<App>.app/Contents/MacOS/<App>"; open <path>` so the stale process dies and the fresh binary launches. Tell the user this once they hit the symptom: 'I don't see any change' = stale process. Do NOT spend cycles diagnosing animation logic / build settings / cache sync until you've confirmed `ps aux | grep <App> | grep -v grep` shows zero pre-rebuild processes.
guidelines.why: Real incident 2026-05-04: Match3 v2/v3/v4 all rebuilt cleanly + cache-synced + committed, but the user kept reporting 'I don't see anything changed'. ps aux showed two stale Match3 processes from earlier in the session — every `open Match3.app` just brought the v2 window forward. The new binaries on disk were correct; the live process wasn't. Burned ~30min of back-and-forth animation work that wasn't actually broken. The fix is a one-line `pkill` before `open`, but it has to be in the loop or the symptom recurs.
examples: RIGHT (validation loop after a rebuild): `pkill -f 'Match3.app/Contents/MacOS/Match3'; open /tmp/catalog-builds/recipe-match3/build/Match3.app` — kills any stale instance, launches the fresh binary.

RIGHT (sanity check before claiming a new build is running): `ps aux | grep -i Match3 | grep -v grep | awk '{print $2, $9}'` — every PID shown is a stale process unless its start time is AFTER your most-recent rebuild timestamp.

WRONG: `open Match3.app` after a rebuild without checking for stale processes. The user sees old UI; you see your new code on disk; both are correct in isolation; the symptom is a stale process, not a code bug.

Sister rule for users: when a catalog app's behavior 'doesn't match the new code', the FIRST debugging step is `ps aux | grep <App>` + check timestamps, not 'is the build correct'. The build is almost always correct — the running process is stale.

guidelines.rule: Falling tiles in tile-based games (match-3, Tetris, sliding-tile, Bejeweled, Threes) MUST deform: stretch vertically (sx≈0.92, sy≈1.12) while falling, squash horizontally (sx≈1.18, sy≈0.86) for ~130ms when they land. This is principle #1 of the 12 Disney principles (squash-and-stretch) — the difference between 'graphic moves' and 'candy lands.' Tiles that scale uniformly or just translate feel weightless and stickered onto the board. The squash is volume-preserving (sx*sy≈1.0).
guidelines.why: Real Candy Crush production animations validated 2026-05-04 via web research + Match3 catalog reference rebuild (v6). Without squash-stretch the user said 'they feel like pngs that are just being animated.' With it the tiles read as having weight.
examples: RIGHT: phaseScales(.falling) returns (0.92, 1.12); phaseScales(.squashing) returns (1.18, 0.86); driven by AnimationIntensity.fallStretch and .squashAmount.
RIGHT (with AnimationIntensity): use the `effects.animation-intensity` Lego component — one struct field controls both stretch and squash, presets handle game pace.
WRONG: tiles use a uniform scale (1.0,1.0) the entire fall; reads as weightless icons sliding.

guidelines.rule: Match clears (or any 'item disappears with feedback' moment) MUST bulge before they shrink — anticipation (12 principles #4) + follow-through (#5). Sequence: (a) scale up 1.20–1.30× over 60–100ms with easeOut, (b) shrink to 0 + fade with easeIn over 80–110ms. A flat fade (just opacity 1→0) feels mechanical; bulge-shrink feels satisfying. The eye registers 'something is about to happen' (bulge) BEFORE the disappearance (shrink). The whole motion takes ~180ms.
guidelines.why: v3/v4/v5 of the catalog Match3 used flat fades and the user reported 'animations are lacking, feels flat.' v6 with bulge-shrink read as 'satisfying pop.' Universal animation principle — applies to card flips, item pickups, balloon pops, any binary disappear event.
examples: RIGHT: withAnimation(.easeOut(duration: 0.08)) { phase = .bulging }; sleep 80ms; withAnimation(.easeIn(duration: 0.10)) { phase = .shrinking }; commit clear after shrink.
RIGHT (with AnimationIntensity): bulgeAmount=0.25, bulgeDuration=0.08, shrinkDuration=0.11 are the standard preset.
WRONG: withAnimation(.easeOut) { tile.opacity = 0; tile.scale = 0 }  — single-phase fade-shrink, no anticipation, feels mechanical.

guidelines.rule: Tile-based games MUST use DIFFERENT animation curves for different phases. Bulging = .easeOut(0.08) (fast pop). Shrinking = .easeIn(0.10) (accelerate as it disappears). Landing = .spring(response:0.18, dampingFraction:0.45) (bouncy). Idle restore = .spring(0.20, 0.78) (settle). One global animation curve for everything reads as 'boring spring on everything' — different timings per phase is what separates 'alive' from 'sterile.'
guidelines.why: Real Candy Crush uses different curves per phase (researched 2026-05-04). v3/v4/v5 of catalog Match3 used one global spring; user feedback was 'feels flat.' v6 with per-phase curves was the first one that 'felt like candy.' Universal — applies to any squash-stretch animated UI.
examples: RIGHT: animationFor(.bulging) returns .easeOut(0.08); animationFor(.shrinking) returns .easeIn(0.10); animationFor(.squashing) returns .spring(response:0.18, dampingFraction:0.45). Apply each via .animation(animationFor(phase), value: phase) on the tile.
WRONG: withAnimation(.spring) wrapping the entire match-resolve cycle — every phase rides one curve, feels uniform.

guidelines.rule: Game generators creating tile-based or arcade games SHOULD use the `effects.animation-intensity` Lego component instead of hardcoding squash/bulge/spring magic numbers in ContentView. Pick a preset based on game pace: `.subtle` for productivity-flavored puzzles (crossword/word/sudoku/utility), `.standard` for mechanics-driven games (match-3/minesweeper/breakout), `.punchy` for arcade-action (asteroids/pacman/plants-vs-zombies/kids-games). One preset switch rescales every effect together; individual fields can be overridden for fine-tuning. Stamp the preset choice into a `// AnimationIntensity: subtle` comment near the property declaration so it's scannable.
guidelines.why: Without a shared knob struct, tuning means hunting for magic numbers across the View; copying numbers from one game to another forgets to scale them for pace. AnimationIntensity centralizes the tuning surface. Filed 2026-05-04 after Match3 v6 work + user request 'might almost be nice to define controls that allow us to dial up and down some of this stuff so we can quickly tune a given game or app.'
examples: RIGHT: `private let intensity: AnimationIntensity = .standard  // AnimationIntensity: standard (match-3 = mechanics-driven feedback)` then use `intensity.bulgeAmount`, `intensity.flashScale`, etc. throughout the View.
RIGHT (override individual): `var i = AnimationIntensity.standard; i.flashScale = 0.5; i.flashOpacity = 0.4 // dial flashes back further` and assign to `intensity`.
WRONG: hardcoded `.scaleEffect(1.25)`, `.font(.system(size: 92))`, `.spring(response: 0.18, dampingFraction: 0.45)` scattered across the View — impossible to retune as a unit.

guidelines.rule: Per-event flash overlays (sparkle for 3-match, burst for 4-match, lightning for 5-match — and any equivalent 'event happened here' visual) should be SCALED DOWN and SEMI-TRANSPARENT, not full-size solid graphics. Rule of thumb: glyph size ~60% of cell size, opacity 0.4–0.6, halo radius modest. Overly large/solid flash overlays read as 'sticker pasted on top of the gameplay' rather than 'effect emanating from the gameplay.' Real production overlays are accents, not centerpieces. Avoid giant text labels like '⚡ 5-MATCH ⚡' — the game-mechanic feedback (score increment, board change) is the centerpiece; the flash is punctuation.
guidelines.why: User feedback 2026-05-04 on catalog Match3 v6.3: 'the animation with the star and the lightning bolt are a bit too big and should probably be a little more transparent.' v6.4 flashes use scale=0.6 + opacity=0.55 + halo=18, removed text labels — and the flashes still register but no longer dominate. AnimationIntensity captures this as `flashScale: 0.6, flashOpacity: 0.55` in the standard preset.
examples: RIGHT: Image(systemName: "sparkles").font(.system(size: 56 * intensity.flashScale, weight: .black)).foregroundStyle(color.opacity(intensity.flashOpacity)).shadow(color: color.opacity(intensity.flashOpacity * 0.8), radius: intensity.flashHaloRadius)
RIGHT: NO 'YOU DID IT' / '5-MATCH' text labels — the score increment + board change communicates the event.
WRONG: opaque 96pt bolt.fill + 150pt circle + 60pt halo + giant Text('⚡ 5-MATCH ⚡') label centered on top of the board. Reads as 'graphic asset' not 'effect.'

guidelines.rule: Game-flavor mini-apps (arcade, match-3, card, puzzle, board games — anything in the 'game' bucket) SHOULD use the `effects.sound-engine` Lego component instead of building their own AVAudioPlayer plumbing. SoundEngine provides pooled players (low-latency overlap), debounced playback, music fade in/out, persistent UserDefaults mute, semantic SFX enum (match3Pop / arcadeLaser / cardSlide / etc.), and intensity presets (.subtle/.standard/.punchy/.soft). 25 CC0 SFX from Kenney are bundled in `assets/sfx/*.m4a`. Generated apps should declare `@ObservedObject private var sound = SoundEngine.shared` and call `sound.play(.match3Pop)`. Add `resources: [.copy("SFX")]` to the SwiftPM target.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 after building SoundEngine component for catalog Match3 v7. User feedback: 'sound effects really add to the overall game experience.' Without a shared engine, every game reinvents pool management, debouncing, mute toggle, music fades — and gets 2-3 of them wrong (jarring volumes, no fade, plays on every render).
examples: RIGHT: import effects.sound-engine; @ObservedObject private var sound = SoundEngine.shared; on match-clear: sound.play(.match3Pop, pitchSemitones: cascadeDepth * 2)
RIGHT (mute button): Button { sound.muted.toggle() } label: { Image(systemName: sound.muted ? "speaker.slash.fill" : "speaker.wave.2.fill") } — required by audio-always-mute-toggle rule.
WRONG: build AVAudioPlayer pool ad-hoc in ContentView; reinvent debouncing; forget mute persistence; skip music fades.

guidelines.rule: Any game with sound MUST expose a visible, persistent mute toggle in the always-visible UI (not hidden in a settings menu). Default state: unmuted at modest volume (intensity.standard.sfxVolume = 0.65). Mute state MUST persist across app restarts via UserDefaults. Use SF Symbols `speaker.wave.2.fill` (unmuted) / `speaker.slash.fill` (muted). Optional but recommended: bind to keyboard shortcut 'M'. SoundEngine.muted handles the persistence — just observe `sound.muted` for the icon swap.
guidelines.why: Universal UX rule. Users tab between games, take calls, share screens. Burying mute deep in a settings menu means the next time they alt-tab in, the SFX surprise everyone in the room. Filed 2026-05-04 alongside SoundEngine ship — incorporated as default behavior in the engine itself but the visible-button is a View-level requirement that can't be enforced by the Lego.
examples: RIGHT: speaker icon in the bottom controls bar, always visible. Tap → mute persists.
RIGHT: keyboardShortcut("m", modifiers: []) for power-users + .help() tooltip.
WRONG: mute hidden in a 'Preferences' menu / hamburger / kebab. Surfacing-cost too high.

guidelines.rule: For match-3 / cascade / chain-reaction games, pitch-shift the SFX +2 semitones per cascade depth (depth 0 = neutral, depth 1 = +2, depth 2 = +4, etc.). Creates the satisfying ascending arpeggio Candy Crush is famous for. Use `sound.play(sfx, pitchSemitones: depth * 2)`. SoundEngine supports this via `enableRate = true` on the underlying AVAudioPlayer pool. Cap at depth 6 (= +12 semitones = double pitch) — beyond that AVAudioPlayer artifacts become obvious. Apply same pattern to: combo counters in arcade games (each combo bumps pitch), enemy-streak-kills, multi-line-clear in Tetris.
guidelines.why: Real Candy Crush production behavior validated 2026-05-04 via web research + Match3 v7 implementation. Without pitch-shift, every cascade pop sounds identical → user can't tell they're chaining. With pitch-shift, the ear hears 'thing 1, thing 2, thing 3' as ascending tones and the cascade feels satisfying. Universal — applies to any game with chainable events.
examples: RIGHT: sound.play(.match3Pop, pitchSemitones: cascadeDepth * 2) — direct swap = neutral, first cascade = +2, second = +4, etc.
RIGHT (combos): sound.play(.arcadeExplosion, pitchSemitones: min(comboCount, 6) * 2)
WRONG: same flat tone on every cascade pop — user can't tell they're chaining.

guidelines.rule: Sound effects must fire on DISCRETE GAME EVENTS (match resolved, button tapped, enemy hit, level up), NOT inside SwiftUI render or `body` evaluation. SoundEngine debounces internally (30ms window per SFX), but if you call `sound.play(.x)` from a `.onAppear` / `body` getter / @State observer that fires on every render, you'll hammer the engine — and the debounce will silently drop most of them but the ones that DO play will be misaligned with what the user did. Place SFX calls inside the action handlers: `Button(action: { sound.play(.uiButtonTap); ...real action... })` or inside the engine-event loop where matches resolve.
guidelines.why: Same anti-pattern as 'wrote tile content into ForEach Text() body' — render-time side effects = unpredictable. Filed 2026-05-04 after wiring Match3: original instinct was to call sound.play(.match3Pop) inside the `flashView` ViewBuilder. That would have fired on every flash render, not just when the flash was created. Moved into the cascade-loop where events.kind is known once per resolution.
examples: RIGHT: in the cascade loop where match events resolve, exactly once per event: `for event in previewEvents { sound.play(matchSFX(for: event.kind), pitchSemitones: event.cascadeDepth * 2) }`
WRONG: `var body: some View { Text("\(score)").onAppear { sound.play(.scoreTick) } }` — fires every render.

WRONG: `.onChange(of: engine.score) { sound.play(.coinPickup) }` triggers on EVERY score change including the +30 from a 3-match → could fire 6+ times in a single cascade. Either debounce upstream or fire on the discrete match event, not the score derivative.

guidelines.rule: Same pattern as AnimationIntensity: pick a SoundIntensity preset by game pace. `.subtle` (sfxVol=0.35, musicVol=0.15) for productivity-flavored puzzles where audio is light punctuation. `.standard` (0.65/0.35) for mechanics-driven games (match-3, minesweeper). `.punchy` (0.85/0.55) for arcade-action (asteroids, pacman, plants-vs-zombies, kids/casual). `.soft` (0.5/0.45) for relaxing/idle/ambient (solitaire, pattern matchers). Stamp the choice in a comment near the SoundEngine declaration: `// SoundIntensity: standard (match-3 mechanics-driven feedback)`.
guidelines.why: Without a shared knob struct, generators copy-paste a volume float and forget to scale for pace. Match-3 at .punchy is too loud; arcade at .subtle disappears. Presets capture the calibration. Mirrors the AnimationIntensity pattern intentionally so generators learn one mental model.
examples: RIGHT: SoundEngine.shared.intensity = .standard  // SoundIntensity: standard (match-3)
RIGHT (override): var i = SoundIntensity.standard; i.sfxVolume = 0.5; i.musicVolume = 0.25; SoundEngine.shared.intensity = i
WRONG: hardcoded `sound.play(.match3Pop, volume: 0.7)` everywhere — impossible to retune as a unit.

guidelines.rule: Background music tracks MUST fade in (1.0–1.5s) and fade out (1.0s) — NEVER hard-cut. Hard cuts are jarring and feel like a glitch. SoundEngine.loop(_:fadeIn:) handles this: `sound.loop(.puzzleAmbient, fadeIn: 1.5)`. Track switches: stopMusic(fadeOut: 0.3) THEN loop(.next, fadeIn: 1.0) — for a clean crossfade-via-stop. App-quit / sheet-dismiss / scene-change: stopMusic(fadeOut: 1.0) before transition. SFX are exempt — they're already short, fade is built into the sound itself.
guidelines.why: Universal audio production rule. Hard cuts on long-form audio = listener notices the seam = breaks immersion. Filed 2026-05-04 with SoundEngine ship — the engine enforces fade defaults so hard cuts only happen if a generator explicitly passes 0.
examples: RIGHT: sound.loop(.puzzleAmbient, fadeIn: 1.5) on game start; sound.stopMusic(fadeOut: 1.0) on game-over screen.
WRONG: musicPlayer.play() / musicPlayer.stop() without fade. Listener hears the abrupt cut.

guidelines.rule: Progression-flavored games (match-3, arcade-with-waves, puzzles-with-difficulty-tiers, runners, idle clickers, platformers with progressing stages) MUST use the `effects.level-manager` Lego component instead of inventing target-score / progression math from scratch. LevelManager provides: difficulty curves (.casual 1.5× / .standard 1.4× / .hardcore 1.3× / .accelerating / .custom), per-level moves budget, suggestedPaletteSize / suggestedSpawnRateMultiplier / suggestedAnimationIntensity helpers, persistent currentLevel via UserDefaults, and a celebration window (`celebratingLevelUp` / `celebrationProgress`) for the banner + SFX. Wire it as `@StateObject private var levels = LevelManager(gameID: "<game>", baseTarget: <num>, baseMovesAllowed: <num>, curve: .standard)`.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 alongside LevelManager ship. Without a shared component, every progression game writes ad-hoc 'targetScore *= 1.5' arithmetic, forgets to persist current level, and has no celebration moment — users finish level 1, see the same screen, and lose engagement. LevelManager + the celebration window turn 'beat target' into a reward.
examples: RIGHT: @StateObject private var levels = LevelManager(gameID: "match3", baseTarget: 1500, baseMovesAllowed: 30, curve: .standard); HUD reads `Text("LEVEL \(levels.currentLevel)")` and `Text("\(levels.currentTarget)")`. On target hit: `levels.advance()`.
RIGHT (UI): scaleEffect on level pill = `1.0 + levels.celebrationProgress * 0.20`; level-up banner opacity = `Double(levels.celebrationProgress)`.
RIGHT (palette growth): `engine.symbols = Array(["A","B","C","D","E","F","G","H"].prefix(levels.suggestedPaletteSize))` — palette starts at 5 colors, grows by +1 every 5 levels, capped at 8.
WRONG: hardcoded `private let targetScore = 1500`, no progression. User beats it, sees nothing change, exits app, never returns.
WRONG: ad-hoc `if score >= 1500 { score = 0; targetScore = 2500 }` — no curve, no persistence, no celebration.

guidelines.rule: When a player advances a level, the UI MUST acknowledge it with a 1-3 second celebration: (a) a prominent 'LEVEL N' banner that inflates and fades, (b) a level-up SFX (SoundEngine.play(.levelUp) — pitch-shift +2 semitones for stronger effect), (c) a brief scale/glow pulse on the level pill / score readout, (d) a tactile pause (~0.5–1s) before the new board / wave is presented. Skipping the celebration means the player isn't told they accomplished something — and the dopamine hit of progression is the entire reason to play. LevelManager provides `celebratingLevelUp: Bool` and `celebrationProgress: CGFloat` (0→1→0 across the window) — bind animations to these.
guidelines.why: Universal game-design principle. Filed 2026-05-04 with LevelManager ship. Without celebration, a level-up feels like a level-down — the user hits the target and the game just changes the number. With celebration (banner + SFX + pulse), the user FEELS the win. Match3 v8 implementation: level pill scaleEffect(1.0 + celebrationProgress * 0.20), banner Text scaleEffect(0.7 + celebrationProgress * 0.6) + opacity(celebrationProgress), levelUp SFX with +2 pitch.
examples: RIGHT: levels.advance() triggers celebrationProgress ramp 0→1 over 0.5s, plateau 0.5s, 1→0 over 2s. View binds banner opacity/scale to celebrationProgress; SFX plays at the start.
RIGHT (audio sequence): on target hit → sound.play(.victory) → 450ms pause → sound.play(.levelUp, pitchSemitones: 2) → levels.advance() triggers banner.
WRONG: levels.advance() called silently with no UI reaction. Player isn't sure if anything happened.
WRONG: instant snap-cut to next level (no banner, no SFX, no delay). Feels like a glitch.

guidelines.rule: When a player fails a level (out of moves, lives depleted, time up), the game MUST allow replay of the SAME level — never auto-demote to a lower level or back to L1. Save the player's progression at all times. RESTART buttons should reset score/moves WITHIN the current level, not bump levels.currentLevel down. Players who hit a hard level will replay it many times; demoting them would be a punishment that destroys engagement. The only way to drop a level should be a deliberate user action (e.g. a 'Reset Progress' settings toggle, never the in-game RESTART button).
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 alongside LevelManager. Hardcoded into LevelManager API: `replayCurrentLevel()` is a no-op on persistence; only `reset()` (explicit reset-progress action) resets currentLevel to 1. Universal UX rule — applied incorrectly, players quit the game forever after one rage-fail.
examples: RIGHT: RESTART button rebuilds engine, refreshes moves to levels.currentMovesAllowed, but does NOT touch levels.currentLevel.
RIGHT: 'Reset Progress' in settings menu calls levels.reset() — opt-in only.
WRONG: RESTART resets currentLevel to 1. Player who beat 5 levels and rage-quit on L6 has to redo all 5.

guidelines.rule: Higher levels should feel different across MULTIPLE dimensions, not just 'higher target number.' For tile/match games: grow the symbol palette (LevelManager.suggestedPaletteSize: 5→6 at L5, 7 at L10, 8 at L15), shrink moves budget if pacing demands it (.accelerating curve), bump animation intensity (LevelManager.suggestedAnimationIntensity: 'subtle' L1-4 → 'standard' L5-9 → 'punchy' L10+). For arcade games: spawn-rate (LevelManager.suggestedSpawnRateMultiplier: 1.0× → 3.0× capped at L20+), enemy variety, projectile speed, intermittent boss waves at L5/L10/L15. The TARGET getting larger is just one knob — alone, it produces 'same game, takes longer.' Multi-knob progression produces 'this level FEELS different.'
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 with LevelManager ship. Real Candy Crush / Bejeweled / etc. grow many things per level (board shape, special-tile generation rate, blockers, mission types). Reference apps in the catalog should at minimum grow palette + intensity for tile games, spawn-rate + intensity for arcade games. LevelManager exposes the suggested helpers so generators don't have to invent them.
examples: RIGHT (match-3): L1: 5 colors, .standard intensity. L5: 6 colors, .standard. L10: 7 colors, .punchy. L15: 8 colors, .punchy. Each level feels harder for a NEW reason.
RIGHT (arcade): L1: 2 enemies, slow projectiles, .standard intensity. L5: 4 enemies, faster projectiles, .standard. L10: 6 enemies + occasional bomb-spawn, .punchy.
WRONG: every level identical except target=basicTarget*1.4^N. Player perceives 'same game just slower to finish' — no surprise, no novelty, drops off.

guidelines.rule: Choose LevelCurve by intended play session length: `.casual` (1.5× per level, fast ramp) for short kid-friendly sessions where players should reach high levels in 10-15 minutes. `.standard` (1.4×) for mechanics-driven games (match-3, minesweeper) where 30-60 min sessions are typical. `.hardcore` (1.3×, slow ramp) for endurance/idle/score-attack games designed for long-term engagement. `.accelerating` (gentle 1.3× early, climbs to 1.6× by L20+) for games that should onboard easily but become a real challenge — typical for puzzle franchises. `.custom` for games with non-monotonic difficulty (boss levels, breather levels, jumps).
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04. Same generator-mental-model pattern as AnimationIntensity / SoundIntensity — pick a preset by game pace, not by inventing arithmetic. Wrong curve = engagement disaster: hardcore curve on a casual game = players quit at L4; casual curve on hardcore = players hit L99 in an hour and have nothing left.
examples: RIGHT: LevelManager(gameID: "kid-game", curve: .casual) — fast progression, frequent level-up celebrations.
RIGHT: LevelManager(gameID: "match3", curve: .standard) — balanced.
RIGHT: LevelManager(gameID: "score-attack", curve: .hardcore) — slow ramp.
WRONG: pick a curve based on coin flip / didn't think about pace; ship a casual-curve match-3 (player ceiling-hits at L20) or hardcore-curve casual game (player gives up at L4).

guidelines.rule: Persist `currentLevel` (the structural progression) via UserDefaults. Do NOT persist `currentScore` (the within-level progress) — it should reset on app launch. Players don't expect to resume a partially-played level mid-game; they expect to come back, see their level number, and start that level fresh. LevelManager handles the level persistence under key 'LevelManager.<gameID>.currentLevel'. SoundEngine.muted is also persisted (separately). Anything else (current score, board state, current cascade depth, hint pair) is ephemeral.
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04 with LevelManager. Saving mid-level state is high-effort + low-value: requires Codable engine state, can desync if engine impl changes, players have to mentally context-switch back into a half-finished puzzle. Saving level-only is what users expect from casual-game UX.
examples: RIGHT: LevelManager persists currentLevel automatically. App launches → onAppear sets engine.symbols based on suggestedPaletteSize, moves = currentMovesAllowed, status = 'Level N — target M'. Score starts at 0.
WRONG: persist current Match3Engine state across launches. Brittle, annoying for users, breaks when engine model evolves.

guidelines.rule: Personal best / high-score should be surfaced in the GAME-OVER (or run-summary) panel, NOT in the always-visible in-play HUD. During active play, in-play high-score chrome (BEST pills, live 'X to beat' / '+N ahead' deltas) is visual noise that distracts from the actual gameplay — players are mid-decision and don't want a constant comparison ticker. The end-of-run panel is when the comparison is meaningful: it's a single moment, the player has earned their score, and seeing 'Best: 12,450' alongside 'Your run: 11,200' creates the natural motivation to play again. Use HighScoreManager.commit(score:) at game-over → it returns true on a new best, fires celebrationProgress for a 'NEW BEST!' flourish on the panel. The in-play HUD shows ONLY the active counters (score / level / moves / time).
guidelines.why: Filed 2026-05-04. Initial implementation v9 put BEST + live delta + 'NEW BEST!' chrome in the always-visible HUD. User feedback: 'why did you go so overboard with the highscore thing? this is a super ugly header. have you seen a high score on a game, it shouldn't show up until the game is done so the user doesn't get so distracted.'
